articles.title: The Value Axis: Language Models Encode Whether They're on the Right Track
articles.summary: We investigate whether language models internally track the value of their current trajectory, defined as the likelihood that their ongoing strategy will achieve their goals. Using synthetic, in-context reinforcement learning data, we construct a "value" axis for Qwen3-8B. We find that activations along this axis distinguish between high vs. low verbalized confidence, rollouts without and with backtracking, and correct vs. corrupted code. Steering towards high value causally suppresses self-correction and reduces explanatory verbosity, while steering towards low value induces backtracking and exploration. We demonstrate that direct preference optimization (DPO) can increase the internal value of rewarded behaviors (e.g. use a certain word), causing the model to act more confidently after exhibiting them. Finally, we apply the value axis to study in-the-wild settings. For example, we find that Qwen assigns low value to politically sensitive chat queries after post-training and that supervised fine-tuning increases internal confidence within the training domain. Our results suggest that language models linearly encode an estimate of expected goal success that modulates their confidence in pursuing a direction.
articles.ranking_reason: Academic paper on LLM internals investigating value tracking and goal alignment. Highly relevant to large language models (0.95) and AI safety/alignment (0.75) interests. Strong research contribution.
articles.summary_text: Researchers discovered that language models like Qwen3-8B internally encode a "value axis"—a measurable pattern in neural activations that reflects whether the model's current strategy is on track to achieve its goals. By mapping this value signal from reinforcement learning data, the team could identify and manipulate this hidden representation. This finding suggests language models develop interpretable internal value judgments similar to how humans assess progress toward objectives.
articles.why_matters: Understanding how models internally represent value and goal progress is crucial for AI alignment, interpretability, and developing more reliable rewa

articles.title: T-Rex: Tactile-Reactive Dexterous Manipulation
articles.summary: The ability to react dynamically to tactile signals has long been considered crucial to agile human-level dexterity. Yet contemporary learning-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation generally either overlook the tactile modality or are limited to encoders with static cues, due in part to the scarcity of diverse training data and standardized evaluation, architectural constraints in current VLA models, and limitations of static tactile encoders. In this paper, we push the frontier of tactile-reactive manipulation by addressing all of these limitations. We propose a large-scale, 100-hour tactile-rich dataset collected via a novel, data-efficient recipe that prioritizes elementary motor primitives. To effectively exploit naturally high-frequency touch signals without sacrificing the existing capabilities of existing VLAs, we introduce a variable-rate Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture equipped with a novel temporal tactile VQ-VAE encoder. We demonstrate the effectiveness of tactile-reactive policies on 12 manipulation tasks requiring delicate force control and deformable object manipulation, achieving over 30% higher average success rate than the strongest baseline.
articles.ranking_reason: Addresses multimodal AI (Vision-Language-Action models at 0.85 interest) and robotics. Academic paper on dexterous manipulation combines relevant interests, though robotics is lower priority (0.40).
articles.summary_text: T-Rex addresses a critical gap in robotic manipulation by integrating dynamic tactile feedback into Vision-Language-Action models, which traditionally ignore or statically encode touch signals. The system enables robots to react in real-time to tactile cues during manipulation tasks, mimicking human dexterity. This reactive capability significantly improves performance on complex manipulation tasks that require dynamic adjustment.
articles.why_matters: Bridging tactile sensing into multimodal VLAs is essential for achieving human-level robotic dexterity and expanding AI systems beyond visual-only con

articles.title: Human Universal Grasping
articles.summary: Humans can grasp objects effortlessly, whereas multi-fingered robots are far from this level of generality. We argue that the most natural source of robot grasping data is from humans, who pick up thousands of objects every day. We present HUG, a flow-matching model that generates diverse human grasps for any user-specified object in a single RGB-D image captured from a stereo camera. Using smart glasses, we first collect 1M-HUGs, an egocentric dataset of human grasps spanning 1M frames (27.8 hrs) and 6,707 object instances across 41 buildings. Next, to model the distribution of natural human grasps, our novel flow-matching model fuses RGB and depth observations to output a grasp parameterized by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose. Predicted grasps can be retargeted to various robot hands, enabling zero-shot grasping in everyday scenes. To standardize evaluation, we build a new simulated benchmark, HUG-Bench, of 90 unseen objects from five geometric categories and various sizes, with metric-scale 3D meshes. We evaluate HUG in the real world on the 30-object test set of HUG-Bench across multiple stereo cameras, robot embodiments, and household environments. HUG outperforms the state-of-the-art grasping baselines by +23% and +34% on our challenging object set. Code, data, benchmark, checkpoints, and an interactive demo are released on our website: https://grasping.io/
articles.ranking_reason: Addresses robotics and grasping, which is peripherally related to reader's interests (robotics at 0.40). However, the focus on physical robot manipulation rather than AI/ML systems limits relevance to core interests.
articles.summary_text: HUG leverages human grasping behavior as training data, using a flow-matching model to generate diverse and generalizable grasp strategies across thousands of object categories. By learning directly from human demonstrations rather than robot-specific data, the approach achieves unprecedented generality in multi-fingered robotic grasping. The method bridges the gap between human manual dexterity and robot capabilities.
articles.why_matters: Learning robot manipulation from human data represents a scalable paradigm shift for training generalist robotic systems without task-specific enginee

articles.title: Context-Aware RL for Agentic and Multimodal LLMs
articles.summary: Large language models (LLMs) often fail when answering requires identifying a small but decisive piece of evidence within a long or complex context, such as a single line in a tool trace or a subtle detail in an image. We propose ContextRL, a context-aware reinforcement learning (RL) method that improves long-horizon reasoning and multimodal performance through an \emph{indirect} auxiliary objective. Instead of supervising only the final answer, ContextRL presents the model with a query, an answer, and two highly similar contexts, and rewards it for selecting the context that supports the query--answer pair, thereby encouraging fine-grained grounding. We construct contrastive context data in two domains: for coding agents, trajectories serve as contexts, yielding 1k pairs built via condition filtering; for multimodal reasoning, images serve as contexts, yielding 7K pairs built via generative editing and similarity search. ContextRL achieves average gains of +2.2% over standard GRPO on 5 long-horizon benchmarks, and +1.8% across 12 diverse visual question answering benchmarks. To disentangle the effect of the proposed objective from that of additional data, we compare against data-augmentation baselines that repurpose the same contrastive contexts as standard query--context--answer examples. These baselines provide little to no improvement, showing that the gains arise from the proposed context-selection objective rather than from the contrastive data alone.
articles.ranking_reason: Directly addresses large language models (0.95 interest), agents & autonomous systems (0.80), multimodal AI (0.85), and reinforcement learning (0.60). The focus on context-aware RL for agentic LLMs aligns strongly with multiple top interests.
articles.summary_text: ContextRL is a reinforcement learning method designed to help LLMs better identify critical evidence within large or complex contexts, such as specific lines in tool traces or subtle image details. The approach trains models to focus on and extract the small but decisive pieces of information needed to answer questions correctly. This addresses a fundamental failure mode where LLMs struggle despite having access to the necessary information.
articles.why_matters: Improving LLM context processing is essential for deploying agentic AI systems that must reliably extract relevant information from multimodal inputs

articles.title: BRDFusion: Physics Meets Generation for Urban Scene Inverse Rendering
articles.summary: Inverse rendering of urban scenes from captured videos enables numerous applications, including content creation and autonomous driving simulation. Physically-based rendering methods follow and control lighting physics, but suffer from reconstruction and rendering artifacts. While generative models produce realistic videos, they offer limited consistency and controllability. We present BRDFusion, a unified framework that combines two complementary models for inverse and forward rendering. Specifically, BRDFusion recovers explicit, consistent scene properties with physical modeling and alleviates optimization ambiguity with generative priors. During forward rendering, the physical model provides controllable rendering from the scene configuration, and the generative model denoises and fixes artifacts. Therefore, our method produces high-quality videos while allowing precise control, outperforming baselines in real and synthetic scenes. Moreover, BRDFusion supports novel-view relighting, night simulation, and dynamic object insertion/editing. Project page: https://shigon255.github.io/brdfusion-page/
articles.ranking_reason: Combines computer vision (0.70 interest) with generative AI and physics-based rendering. While touching on vision and generation, it does not strongly address core LLM, agents, or multimodal interests, making it moderate signal.
articles.summary_text: BRDFusion combines physics-based rendering with generative models to improve inverse rendering of urban scenes from video, enabling realistic reconstruction of materials, lighting, and geometry. The hybrid approach leverages generative models to overcome typical artifacts and reconstruction failures in purely physics-based methods. This enables practical applications in content creation and autonomous driving simulation.
articles.why_matters: Bridging physics simulation with generative AI creates more realistic and controllable 3D scene reconstruction, critical for creating high-fidelity tr

Using synthetic, in-context reinforcement learning data, we construct a "value" axis for Qwen3-8B. We find that activations along this axis distinguish between high vs. low verbalized confidence, rollouts without and with backtracking, and correct vs. corrupted code. Steering towards high value causally suppresses self-correction and reduces explanatory verbosity, while steering towards low value induces backtracking and exploration. We demonstrate that direct preference optimization (DPO) can increase the internal value of rewarded behaviors (e.g. use a certain word), causing the model to act more confidently after exhibiting them. Finally, we apply the value axis to study in-the-wild settings. For example, we find that Qwen assigns low value to politically sensitive chat queries after post-training and that supervised fine-tuning increases internal confidence within the training domain. Our results suggest that language models linearly encode an estimate of expected goal success that modulates their confidence in pursuing a direction.

Using synthetic reinforcement learning data, they demonstrated that specific activation patterns reliably reflect whether the model is on track to succeed. This reveals an interpretable internal mechanism for tracking solution progress.

Yet contemporary learning-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation generally either overlook the tactile modality or are limited to encoders with static cues, due in part to the scarcity of diverse training data and standardized evaluation, architectural constraints in current VLA models, and limitations of static tactile encoders. articles.summary: Large language models (LLMs) often fail when answering requires identifying a small but decisive piece of evidence within a long or complex context, such as a single line in a tool trace or a subtle detail in an image. We propose ContextRL, a context-aware reinforcement learning (RL) method that improves long-horizon reasoning and multimodal performance through an \emph{indirect} auxiliary objective. articles.summary_text: ContextRL proposes a reinforcement learning method that teaches LLMs to identify critical information within complex contexts, addressing a common failure mode where models miss decisive details in long documents or images. The approach trains agents to focus attention on relevant evidence rather than passively processing entire inputs. This improves performance on tasks requiring precise evidence retrieval and reasoning.
articles.why_matters: Improving LLMs' ability to extract and act on sparse critical information from noisy contexts is essential for reliable agentic AI systems in real-wor

articles.title: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Meta-Analysis Articles from Nature Portfolio
articles.summary:
Meta-analysis is a demanding form of evidence synthesis that combines literature retrieval, PI/ECO-guided study selection, and statistical aggregation. Its structured, verifiable workflow makes it an ideal substrate for evaluating systematic scientific reasoning, yet existing benchmarks lack ground truth across the full retrieval-screening-synthesis pipeline. We introduce MetaSyn, a dataset of 442 expert-curated meta-analyses from Nature Portfolio journals. Each entry pairs a research question with PI/ECO criteria, a retrieval corpus of 140k PubMed articles, verified positive studies, hard negatives that are topically similar but PI/ECO-ineligible, and complete search strategies and date bounds.
  Benchmarking twelve pipeline configurations (nine RAG variants and a protocol-driven agent) reveals a critical screening bottleneck: despite a retrieval ceiling of 90.9% recall at K=200, no system recovers more than 52.7% of ground-truth included literature. Current LLMs fail to reliably separate eligible studies from PI/ECO-failing distractors in pools of comparable topical relevance. Stage-attributed metrics capture where systems succeed and fail; a single end-to-end score does not.
articles.ranking_reason: Directly addresses LLM agents (0.95 interest) and autonomous task execution (0.80 interest). Benchmarking study on complex reasoning tasks is high-quality research with strong methodological focus.
articles.summary_text: This work benchmarks LLM agents on meta-analysis tasks from Nature Portfolio, a rigorous scientific workflow involving literature retrieval, study selection, and statistical synthesis. Meta-analysis provides a structured, verifiable evaluation ground for systematic reasoning that existing benchmarks lack. The benchmark tests whether agents can execute complex, multi-step evidence synthesis comparable to human scientists.
articles.why_matters: Establishing rigorous scientific reasoning benchmarks helps assess whether LLMs can perform genuine knowledge synthesis rather than surface-level patt

Researchers discovered that language models like Qwen3-8B internally encode a "value axis"—a measurable pattern in neural activations that reflects whether the model's current strategy is on track to achieve its goals. By mapping this value signal from reinforcement learning data, the team could identify and manipulate this hidden representation. This finding suggests language models develop interpretable internal value judgments similar to how humans assess progress toward objectives.

T-Rex: Tactile-Reactive Dexterous Manipulation

arxiv.org
T-Rex addresses a critical gap in robotic manipulation by integrating dynamic tactile feedback into Vision-Language-Action models, which traditionally ignore or statically encode touch signals. The system enables robots to react in real-time to tactile cues during manipulation tasks, mimicking human dexterity. This reactive capability significantly improves performance on complex manipulation tasks that require dynamic adjustment.

Human Universal Grasping

arxiv.org
HUG leverages human grasping behavior as training data, using a flow-matching model to generate diverse and generalizable grasp strategies across thousands of object categories. By learning directly from human demonstrations rather than robot-specific data, the approach achieves unprecedented generality in multi-fingered robotic grasping. The method bridges the gap between human manual dexterity and robot capabilities.
Learning robot manipulation from human data represents a scalable paradigm shift for training generalist robotic systems without task-specific enginee
